knowledge.content: Sinclair is the world's all-time leader for international goals scored for men or women with 187 goals, and is one of the most-capped active international footballer with 300 caps.
Along with Cristiano Ronaldo, he is the world's joint all-time leading goalscorer in the history of men's international football with 109 goals scored for Iran.

Ronaldo holds the records for most goals (134) and assists (42) in the Champions League, most goals in the European Championship (14), and is currently tied with Ali Daei for most international goals (109).

FIFA, the international governing body of football, have never released a list detailing the highest goalscorers and do not keep official records;[6][7] in 2020, they recognised Bican, an Austrian-Czech dual international who played between the 1930s and the 1950s,[8] as the record scorer with an estimated 805 goals,[9][10] while CNN, the BBC, France 24, and O Jogo all acknowledge that Bican's tally of 805 includes goals scored for reserve teams and in unofficial international matches.

The album's title is a slight modification of the title of the duo's first major hit, "The Sound of Silence", which originally was released as "The Sounds of Silence". The song had earlier been released in an acoustic version on the album "Wednesday Morning, 3 A.M.", and later on the soundtrack to the movie "The Graduate". Without the knowledge of Paul Simon or Art Garfunkel, electric guitars, bass and drums were overdubbed by Columbia Records staff producer Tom Wilson on June 15, 1965. This new version was released as a single in September 1965, and opens the album.

question: Who invented the telephone and when?
answer: The telephone was invented by Alexander Graham Bell, who was awarded the first patent for the telephone on March 7, 1876. However, Antonio Meucci had developed an early voice communication device before Bell and filed a caveat in 1871. Elisha Gray also independently developed a telephone design around the same time as Bell.

docs.title: Alexander Graham Bell
docs.text: Alexander Graham Bell (March 3, 1847 – August 2, 1922) was a Scottish-American inventor and scientist who is credited with patenting the first practical telephone. Bell filed his patent application on February 14, 1876, and was officially awarded US Patent 174,465 on March 7, 1876. The patent described 'the method of, and apparatus for, transmitting vocal or other sounds telegraphically.' Bell's laboratory notebook entry for March 10, 1876 records the first successful bi-directional transmission of clear speech using his telephone, when he spoke to his assistant Thomas Watson: 'Mr. Watson—Come here—I want to see you.'
docs.evidence_sentence: Bell filed his patent application on February 14, 1876, and was officially awarded US Patent 174,465 on March 7, 1876.
docs.annotation_note: Conferma direttamente Bell come inventore del telefono e la data del brevetto (7 marzo 1876).
docs.extraction: Alexander Graham Bell was awarded the first patent for the telephone on March 7, 1876.
docs.summary: Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone on March 7, 1876, and the first successful voice transmission occurred on March 10, 1876.

docs.title: Antonio Meucci
docs.text: Antonio Santi Giuseppe Meucci (13 April 1808 – 18 October 1889) was an Italian inventor and activist. Born in Florence, he developed a form of voice communication device in 1849, and later, in 1871, filed a caveat (a temporary patent) for a 'sound telegraph'. Unable to pay the $10 fee to renew the caveat, it was allowed to lapse after 1874. In 2002, the United States Congress passed a resolution honoring Meucci's contributions to the invention of the telephone and recognizing that his work preceded that of Alexander Graham Bell.
docs.evidence_sentence: he developed a form of voice communication device in 1849, and later, in 1871, filed a caveat (a temporary patent) for a 'sound telegraph'.

docs.title: Elisha Gray
docs.text: Elisha Gray (August 2, 1835 – January 21, 1901) was an American electrical engineer who co-founded the Western Electric Manufacturing Company. On February 14, 1876, Gray filed a patent caveat for a telephone design at the U.S. Patent Office, the same day Alexander Graham Bell filed his patent application. After a lengthy legal dispute, the patent was awarded to Bell. Gray later claimed that a Patent Office official had given Bell's application priority. Gray and Bell's competing claims resulted in one of the most complex patent disputes in history.

docs.title: Invention of the telephone
docs.text: The invention of the telephone is credited to multiple inventors. Alexander Graham Bell was the first to be granted a patent for the electric telephone in 1876. The historical and technical details surrounding the invention are complex and controversial. The question of who deserves credit for the telephone has been a subject of intense dispute among historians and engineers. Beyond Bell, Gray, and Meucci, German inventor Johann Philipp Reis had also constructed a device capable of transmitting sounds electrically as early as 1861, though his device could not transmit clear speech.

docs.text: A telephone is a telecommunications device that permits two or more users to conduct a conversation when they are too far apart to be heard directly. question: Who wrote Romeo and Juliet and what is it about?
answer: Romeo and Juliet was written by William Shakespeare, believed to have been written around 1594–1596. It is a tragedy about two young star-crossed lovers, Romeo Montague and Juliet Capulet, whose families are engaged in a long-running feud in Verona, Italy. Their secret marriage and desperate attempts to be together ultimately end in both their deaths, which reconciles the feuding families.

Their attempt to escape together fails, and both die tragically.

docs.title: William Shakespeare
docs.text: William Shakespeare (bapt. 26 April 1564 – 23 April 1616) was an English playwright, poet and actor. He is widely regarded as the greatest writer in the English language and the world's greatest dramatist. He is often called England's national poet and the 'Bard of Avon'. His extant works, including collaborations, consist of some 39 plays, 154 sonnets and a few longer poems. Romeo and Juliet is one of his early tragedies and is considered one of his most famous works. His plays have been translated into every major language and are performed more often than those of any other playwright.

question: What is the speed of light and who measured it first?
answer: The speed of light in a vacuum is exactly 299,792,458 metres per second (approximately 300,000 km/s or 186,000 miles per second). The first quantitative measurement of the speed of light was made by Danish astronomer Ole Rømer in 1676 using observations of Jupiter's moon Io. James Bradley provided a more accurate measurement in 1728. The exact value was fixed by definition in 1983 when the metre was redefined.

qa_pairs.context: The speed of light in vacuum, commonly denoted c, is a universal physical constant equal to exactly 299,792,458 metres per second. This exact value was established by the 17th General Conference on Weights and Measures in 1983.
qa_pairs.question: What is the speed of light?

qa_pairs.context: Ole Rømer first demonstrated in 1676 that light travels at a finite speed by studying the apparent motion of Jupiter's moon Io. By timing occultations of Io, he estimated the speed of light as approximately 220,000 km/s—considerably less than the true value, but the first quantitative measurement.
qa_pairs.question: Who first measured the speed of light?

qa_pairs.context: In 1728, English astronomer James Bradley measured the speed of light by observing stellar aberration, obtaining a value of about 301,000 km/s—much closer to the true value than Rømer's estimate.
qa_pairs.question: Who provided the second major measurement of the speed of light?

docs.title: Speed of light
docs.text: The speed of light in vacuum, commonly denoted c, is a universal physical constant equal to exactly 299,792,458 metres per second (approximately 300,000 km/s or 186,000 mi/s). According to the special theory of relativity, c is the upper limit for the speed at which conventional matter, energy, or any signal carrying information can travel through space. In 1983, the metre was redefined in the International System of Units (SI) as the distance travelled by light in vacuum in 1/299,792,458 of a second, making the speed of light in vacuum exact by definition.

docs.title: Ole Rømer
docs.text: Ole Christensen Rømer (25 September 1644 – 19 September 1710) was a Danish astronomer who made the first quantitative measurements of the speed of light. In 1676, Rømer observed that the periods of Jupiter's innermost moon, Io, appeared to be shorter when Earth was approaching Jupiter than when it was receding. He correctly attributed this difference to the varying time it takes light to travel from Jupiter to Earth. His estimate of the speed of light was approximately 220,000 km/s, which, although lower than the actual value, was a groundbreaking achievement in physics.
docs.evidence_sentence: In 1676, Rømer observed that the periods of Jupiter's innermost moon, Io, appeared to be shorter when Earth was approaching Jupiter than when it was receding.

docs.summary: Ole Rømer first measured the speed of light in 1676 by observing Jupiter's moon Io, estimating approximately 220,000 km/s.

docs.title: James Bradley
docs.text: James Bradley FRS (1693 – 13 July 1762) was an English astronomer and served as Astronomer Royal from 1742. In 1728, Bradley discovered stellar aberration, the apparent shift in the positions of stars due to Earth's orbital velocity. By measuring the angle of aberration, he was able to calculate the speed of light as approximately 301,000 km/s—much closer to the true value than Rømer's 1676 estimate. Bradley's measurement remained the most accurate for over a century and established the approximate value of c well before modern precision methods.
docs.evidence_sentence: By measuring the angle of aberration, he was able to calculate the speed of light as approximately 301,000 km/s—much closer to the true value than Rømer's 1676 estimate.

docs.title: Fizeau–Foucault apparatus
docs.text: The Fizeau–Foucault apparatus is an instrument used to measure the speed of light. Hippolyte Fizeau first measured the terrestrial speed of light in 1849 using a rotating toothed wheel, obtaining a value of approximately 313,300 km/s. Léon Foucault improved on this in 1862 using a rotating mirror, obtaining 298,000 km/s—very close to the modern value. Albert Michelson later achieved extremely precise measurements using improved rotating mirror methods between 1877 and 1931.

docs.title: Metre
docs.text: The metre is the fundamental unit of length in the International System of Units (SI). Since 1983, the metre has been defined as the length of the path travelled by light in vacuum during a time interval of exactly 1/299,792,458 of a second. This definition fixes the speed of light to exactly 299,792,458 metres per second. Previously, the metre had been defined in terms of a physical artefact—a platinum-iridium bar kept at the International Bureau of Weights and Measures in Sèvres, France.

question: Who painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling and how long did it take?
answer: The ceiling of the Sistine Chapel was painted by Michelangelo. He worked on the project from 1508 to 1512, a period of approximately four years. Pope Julius II commissioned the work. The ceiling depicts scenes from the Book of Genesis, including the famous 'Creation of Adam'. Michelangelo painted the ceiling largely by himself, working on scaffolding of his own design.

The ceiling is approximately 40 metres long and 14 metres wide, covering over 500 square metres. Michelangelo painted the ceiling by himself without assistance, though he did employ assistants for minor tasks. He began painting in May 1508, and the ceiling was unveiled on 31 October 1512.

question: Who was Marie Curie and what did she discover?
answer: Marie Curie (1867–1934) was a Polish-French physicist and chemist who conducted pioneering research on radioactivity. She discovered two elements: polonium (named after her native Poland) in July 1898 and radium in December 1898, both together with her husband Pierre Curie. She was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize, the first person to win the Nobel Prize twice, and the only person to win the prize in two different sciences—Physics in 1903 and Chemistry in 1911.

docs.text: Polonium is a chemical element with the symbol Po and atomic number 84. It was discovered in July 1898 by Marie Curie and Pierre Curie. docs.title: Radium
docs.text: Radium is a chemical element with the symbol Ra and atomic number 88. It is the sixth element in group 2 of the periodic table, also known as the alkaline earth metals. docs.text: Radioactivity, also known as nuclear decay or radioactive decay, is the process by which an unstable atomic nucleus loses energy by radiation. question: Who built the Eiffel Tower and why?
answer: The Eiffel Tower was designed and built by Gustave Eiffel's engineering company. It was constructed between 1887 and 1889 as the entrance arch for the 1889 World's Fair (Exposition Universelle) held in Paris to celebrate the centennial of the French Revolution. The tower stands 330 metres tall and was the tallest man-made structure in the world for 41 years until the Chrysler Building surpassed it in 1930.

question: Who was the first person to walk on the Moon and when?
answer: Neil Armstrong was the first person to walk on the Moon, on July 20, 1969, during the Apollo 11 mission. He was followed about 20 minutes later by fellow astronaut Buzz Aldrin. The third crew member, Michael Collins, remained in lunar orbit aboard the Command Module. Armstrong's famous words upon stepping onto the surface were: 'That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind.'

Armstrong became the first person to step onto the lunar surface six hours and 39 minutes later.

docs.title: Apollo 11
docs.text: Apollo 11 (July 16–24, 1969) was the American spaceflight that first landed humans on the Moon. Commander Neil Armstrong and lunar module pilot Buzz Aldrin formed the American crew that landed the Apollo Lunar Module Eagle on July 20, 1969, at 20:17 UTC. Armstrong became the first person to step onto the lunar surface six hours and 39 minutes later, on July 21 at 02:56 UTC; Aldrin joined him 19 minutes later. They spent about two and a quarter hours together outside the spacecraft, and collected 47.5 pounds (21.5 kg) of lunar material to bring back to Earth. Command module pilot Michael Collins flew the command module Columbia alone in lunar orbit while they were on the Moon's surface.

question: What is the Great Wall of China and how long is it?
answer: The Great Wall of China is a series of fortifications that were built across the historical northern borders of ancient Chinese states and Imperial China to protect against nomadic invasions. The wall was built over many centuries by various dynasties. A 2012 archaeological survey by the State Administration of Cultural Heritage determined the total length of all sections to be 21,196 kilometres (13,171 miles). The most well-known and best-preserved section was built during the Ming dynasty (1368–1644).

qa_pairs.question: How long is the Great Wall of China?

docs.title: Great Wall of China
docs.text: The Great Wall of China is a series of fortifications that were built across the historical northern borders of ancient Chinese states and Imperial China as protection against various nomadic groups from the Eurasian Steppe. Several walls were built from as early as the 7th century BC, with selective stretches later joined together by Qin Shi Huang (220–206 BC), the first emperor of China. Little of the Qin wall remains. Later on, many successive dynasties built and maintained multiple stretches of border walls. The most well-known sections of the wall were built by the Ming dynasty (1368–1644). A 2012 survey found that the Ming walls measure 8,850 km (5,500 mi), while the total length of all walls across all dynasties measures 21,196 km (13,171 mi).

docs.title: Ming dynasty
docs.text: The Ming dynasty (1368–1644) was the ruling dynasty of China for 276 years. It was the last imperial dynasty of China ruled by ethnic Han Chinese. During the Ming dynasty, the Great Wall of China was extensively rebuilt and extended to its current form. Most of the wall that tourists visit today, including the well-known sections at Badaling and Mutianyu near Beijing, dates from the Ming period. The Ming wall was constructed using fired bricks and stone rather than the earlier tamped earth, making it more durable.

docs.title: Qin Shi Huang
docs.text: Qin Shi Huang (259–210 BC) was the founder of the Qin dynasty and the first emperor of a unified China. He is credited with ordering the construction of the first version of the Great Wall of China, connecting and extending existing walls built by the Warring States. The original Qin walls, built largely of tamped earth and wood, were intended to protect the northern borders from Xiongnu invasions. Very little of the original Qin wall remains today, as it was built from perishable materials.

docs.title: Badaling
docs.text: Badaling is a site north of Juyongguan Pass in Yanqing District, Beijing. It is the most-visited section of the Great Wall of China, receiving around 10 million tourists per year. The Badaling section was built during the Ming dynasty and stands at about 7.8 metres (26 feet) tall and 5 metres (16 feet) wide. It was the first section of the Great Wall to be opened to tourists in 1957. Numerous world leaders and heads of state have visited Badaling, including U.S. presidents Nixon, Reagan, and Clinton.

question: Who wrote the theory of evolution and when?
answer: The theory of evolution by natural selection was primarily developed by Charles Darwin, who published it in his 1859 book 'On the Origin of Species'. Alfred Russel Wallace independently conceived a similar theory and co-presented it with Darwin in 1858. Darwin's theory proposed that species evolve over generations through natural selection, where individuals with favourable traits survive and reproduce more successfully.

qa_pairs.context: Charles Darwin published On the Origin of Species on 24 November 1859. The book introduced the scientific theory that populations evolve over generations through the process of natural selection.
qa_pairs.question: Who developed the theory of evolution by natural selection?

qa_pairs.question: Who else independently developed a theory of evolution at the same time as Darwin?

qa_pairs.context: On the Origin of Species was published on 24 November 1859 by John Murray. The first edition of 1,250 copies sold out on the day of publication. The book's full title is 'On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection, or the Preservation of Favoured Races in the Struggle for Life'.
qa_pairs.question: When was On the Origin of Species published?

docs.title: On the Origin of Species
docs.text: On the Origin of Species (or, more completely, On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection, or the Preservation of Favoured Races in the Struggle for Life) is a work of scientific literature by Charles Darwin that is considered the foundation of evolutionary biology. The book was published on 24 November 1859, by John Murray. The first edition of 1,250 copies sold out the same day. Darwin presents a body of evidence that the diversity of life arose by common descent through a branching pattern of evolution. His theory of natural selection was proposed as the mechanism.

docs.title: Charles Darwin
docs.text: Charles Robert Darwin (12 February 1809 – 19 April 1882) was an English naturalist, geologist and biologist, best known for his contributions to the science of evolution. His proposition that all species of life have descended from common ancestors is now widely accepted and considered a fundamental concept in science. In a joint presentation with Alfred Russel Wallace, he introduced his scientific theory that this branching pattern of evolution resulted from a process that he called natural selection. His 1859 book On the Origin of Species established evolutionary theory.

docs.title: Alfred Russel Wallace
docs.text: Alfred Russel Wallace (8 January 1823 – 7 November 1913) was a British naturalist, explorer, geographer, anthropologist, biologist and illustrator. He is best known for independently conceiving the theory of evolution through natural selection; his 1858 paper on the subject was jointly published with some of Charles Darwin's writings and prompted Darwin to write and publish On the Origin of Species. Darwin and Wallace co-presented their findings at the Linnean Society of London on 1 July 1858.

docs.title: Natural selection
docs.text: Natural selection is the differential survival and reproduction of individuals due to differences in phenotype. It is a key mechanism of evolution, the change in the heritable characteristics of a population over time. Charles Darwin popularised the term 'natural selection' contrasting it with artificial selection. The concept was independently conceived by Darwin and Wallace. Natural selection acts on the phenotype, the characteristics of the organism which actually interact with the environment, but the genetic (heritable) basis of any phenotype that gives that phenotype a reproductive advantage will become more common in a population.

docs.title: Jean-Baptiste Lamarck
docs.text: Jean-Baptiste Pierre Antoine de Monet, Chevalier de Lamarck (1 August 1744 – 18 December 1829) was a French naturalist and biologist. He is recognised as a pioneer of the idea that biological evolution occurs. His theory of 'soft inheritance' proposed that an organism could pass on characteristics acquired during its lifetime to its offspring. Lamarck published his theory of evolution in 1809, fifty years before Darwin's On the Origin of Species, but his mechanism—inheritance of acquired characteristics—was later shown to be incorrect.

question: What is the Titanic and when did it sink?
answer: The RMS Titanic was a British ocean liner operated by the White Star Line. It sank in the North Atlantic Ocean on 15 April 1912 after striking an iceberg during its maiden voyage from Southampton to New York City. Of the estimated 2,224 passengers and crew aboard, more than 1,500 died, making it one of the deadliest peacetime maritime disasters in history. The ship was designed by Thomas Andrews and built at the Harland and Wolff shipyard in Belfast.

question: Where is Mount Everest and how tall is it?
answer: Mount Everest is located in the Mahalangur Himal sub-range of the Himalayas, on the border between Nepal and Tibet (China). It is the highest mountain on Earth above sea level, with a height of 8,848.86 metres (29,031.7 ft) as measured in a 2020 survey by China and Nepal. The first confirmed ascent was made by Edmund Hillary of New Zealand and Tenzing Norgay, a Sherpa from Nepal, on 29 May 1953.

question: What caused World War I and when did it start?
answer: World War I began on 28 July 1914, when Austria-Hungary declared war on Serbia. The immediate trigger was the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary on 28 June 1914 in Sarajevo by Gavrilo Princip, a Bosnian Serb nationalist. The underlying causes included militarism, imperial rivalries, a complex system of alliances, and nationalist tensions across Europe. The war lasted until 11 November 1918.

question: Who was Leonardo da Vinci and what did he paint?
answer: Leonardo da Vinci (1452–1519) was an Italian polymath of the High Renaissance, widely considered one of the greatest painters of all time. He is best known for two paintings: the Mona Lisa, widely regarded as the world's most famous portrait, and The Last Supper, the most reproduced religious painting. Da Vinci was also a sculptor, architect, musician, engineer, scientist, and inventor, keeping extensive notebooks filled with scientific drawings and observations that were centuries ahead of his time.

question: What is DNA and who discovered its structure?
answer: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a molecule that carries the genetic instructions used in the growth, development, functioning and reproduction of all known living organisms. The double helix structure of DNA was discovered in 1953 by James Watson and Francis Crick at the University of Cambridge, based in part on X-ray crystallography work by Rosalind Franklin and Raymond Gosling. Watson, Crick, and Maurice Wilkins were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1962.

The structure explains how DNA encodes genetic information and how it can be copied during cell division.

question: What is the theory of general relativity and who developed it?
answer: The theory of general relativity was developed by Albert Einstein and published in 1915. It is a geometric theory of gravitation that describes gravity not as a force (as Newton had proposed) but as a curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy. Key predictions of the theory include the bending of light by gravity, gravitational time dilation, the existence of black holes, and gravitational waves. The theory replaced Newtonian gravity for extreme conditions and was first confirmed in 1919 when Arthur Eddington observed the bending of light during a solar eclipse.

docs.title: General relativity
docs.text: General relativity, also known as the general theory of relativity, is the geometric theory of gravitation published by Albert Einstein in 1915 and is the current description of gravitation in modern physics. General relativity generalizes special relativity and refines Newton's law of universal gravitation, providing a unified description of gravity as a geometric property of space and time, or four-dimensional spacetime. The curvature of spacetime is directly related to the energy and momentum of whatever matter and radiation are present. The relation is specified by Einstein's field equations.

docs.title: Tests of general relativity
docs.text: The tests of general relativity served as evidence for Einstein's theory of general relativity and have allowed that theory to replace Newtonian gravity. The first major test came during the solar eclipse of May 29, 1919, when Arthur Eddington led an expedition to measure the deflection of starlight by the Sun. The observed bending closely matched Einstein's prediction of 1.75 arcseconds and provided compelling evidence for general relativity. This result made headline news worldwide and established Einstein as an iconic figure.

docs.title: Spacetime
docs.text: In physics, spacetime is any mathematical model that fuses the three dimensions of space and the one dimension of time into a single four-dimensional continuum. The concept was introduced by mathematician Hermann Minkowski in 1908 in the context of special relativity. In general relativity, Einstein showed that spacetime is not flat but curves in the presence of mass and energy, and that this curvature is what we perceive as gravity. The more massive an object, the more it curves the spacetime around it.

docs.title: Gravitational wave
docs.text: Gravitational waves are ripples in the curvature of spacetime that propagate as waves outward from their source. They were predicted by Albert Einstein in 1916 on the basis of his general theory of relativity. For decades gravitational waves were considered a theoretical prediction only. On September 14, 2015, the LIGO observatory made the first direct detection of gravitational waves, produced by the merger of two black holes. This discovery was announced in February 2016 and the scientists involved were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2017.

question: What is the Great Wall of China and why was it built?
answer: The Great Wall of China is a series of fortifications built across the historical northern borders of China. Construction began as early as the 7th century BC during the Western Zhou dynasty, with major building phases during the Qin dynasty (221–206 BC) under Emperor Qin Shi Huang and later during the Ming dynasty (1368–1644). The wall was primarily built to protect Chinese territories from nomadic invasions from the north, particularly from peoples such as the Xiongnu, Mongols, and other steppe peoples. The Ming dynasty wall, the most well-preserved section, stretches over 8,850 km.

Hundreds of thousands of workers, including soldiers, peasants, and prisoners, were conscripted to build the wall under harsh conditions.

question: What is the Sistine Chapel and who painted its ceiling?
answer: The Sistine Chapel is a chapel in the Apostolic Palace in Vatican City and the official residence of the Pope. It is famous for its ceiling painted by Michelangelo between 1508 and 1512, commissioned by Pope Julius II. The ceiling depicts scenes from the Book of Genesis, including the iconic 'Creation of Adam' fresco. Michelangelo also painted 'The Last Judgment' on the altar wall between 1536 and 1541. The chapel takes its name from Pope Sixtus IV, who commissioned its construction between 1473 and 1481, and it is the site where the papal conclave elects new popes.

question: What caused the fall of the Roman Empire?
answer: The fall of the Western Roman Empire in 476 AD is attributed to a combination of factors. Historians cite internal causes such as political instability, military overextension, economic troubles, and the debasement of currency. External pressures included invasions by Germanic tribes (Visigoths, Vandals, Huns, Ostrogoths) who gradually eroded Roman frontiers. The traditional end date is 476 AD, when the Germanic chieftain Odoacer deposed the last Western emperor, Romulus Augustulus. The Eastern Roman Empire (Byzantine Empire) survived for nearly another thousand years until 1453.

question: Who was William Shakespeare and what are his most famous works?
answer: William Shakespeare (1564–1616) was an English playwright, poet, and actor widely regarded as the greatest writer in the English language and the world's greatest dramatist. He was born in Stratford-upon-Avon, England. Shakespeare wrote approximately 37 plays, 154 sonnets, and several longer poems. His most famous plays include Hamlet, Othello, King Lear, Macbeth, A Midsummer Night's Dream, Romeo and Juliet, and The Tempest. He co-owned the Globe Theatre in London, where many of his plays were performed.

docs.title: Globe Theatre
docs.text: The Globe Theatre was a theatre in London associated with William Shakespeare. The original Globe was destroyed by fire in June 1613. A second Globe Theatre was built on the same site by June 1614 and was in use until 1642. question: What is DNA and what role does it play in heredity?
answer: DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is a molecule that carries the genetic instructions for the development, functioning, growth, and reproduction of all known living organisms. It is structured as a double helix, consisting of two strands wound around each other, made up of four nucleotide bases: adenine (A), thymine (T), guanine (G), and cytosine (C). DNA plays a central role in heredity: genes, which are specific sequences of DNA, are passed from parents to offspring and determine inherited traits. The structure of DNA was first described by James Watson and Francis Crick in 1953, based on X-ray crystallography data from Rosalind Franklin.

DNA and ribonucleic acid (RNA) are nucleic acids. Alongside proteins, lipids, and complex carbohydrates (polysaccharides), nucleic acids are one of the four major types of macromolecules that are essential for all known forms of life. The two DNA strands are known as polynucleotides since they are composed of simpler monomeric units called nucleotides.

docs.title: Genetics
docs.text: Genetics is a branch of biology concerned with the study of genes, genetic variation, and heredity in organisms. Genes are segments of DNA that encode information for the production of proteins, which carry out the functions of a cell. Traits are passed from parents to offspring through genes, a process known as inheritance. The field was pioneered by Gregor Mendel in the 19th century, whose experiments with pea plants established the basic principles of heredity. Modern genetics integrates Mendelian inheritance with molecular biology.

docs.title: Nucleic acid double helix
docs.text: A nucleic acid double helix is a structure formed by two complementary strands of nucleic acids—typically DNA—held together by hydrogen bonds between paired bases. The double-helix structure of DNA was proposed by James Watson and Francis Crick in a landmark 1953 paper in Nature, based in part on X-ray crystallography work by Rosalind Franklin and Raymond Gosling. Watson and Crick were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1962 for this discovery, shared with Maurice Wilkins.

docs.title: Rosalind Franklin
docs.text: Rosalind Elsie Franklin (25 July 1920 – 16 April 1958) was a British chemist and X-ray crystallographer whose work was central to the understanding of the molecular structures of DNA, RNA, viruses, coal, and graphite. Her X-ray diffraction images of DNA, particularly Photo 51, were crucial data that contributed to Watson and Crick's 1953 model of the DNA double helix. Franklin's contribution was not fully recognized during her lifetime; she died of cancer in 1958, four years before Watson, Crick, and Wilkins received the Nobel Prize.

question: What is the Amazon rainforest and why is it important?
answer: The Amazon rainforest, also called Amazonia, is a moist broadleaf tropical rainforest in the Amazon biome covering most of the Amazon basin of South America. It covers approximately 5.5 million km² across nine countries, with the largest portion (about 60%) in Brazil. The Amazon is the world's largest tropical rainforest and contains 10% of all species on Earth. It is critically important as the 'lungs of the Earth,' absorbing vast quantities of carbon dioxide, regulating the global climate, and maintaining the water cycle. However, it faces serious threats from deforestation, primarily for agriculture and cattle ranching.

docs.text: The Amazon River is a major river in South America that runs through the Amazon rainforest. It is the largest river by discharge of water in the world, with an average flow of approximately 209,000 cubic metres per second. The Amazon basin drains an area of approximately 7 million km², and the river plays a crucial role in maintaining the hydrology of the Amazon rainforest. The river is home to thousands of fish species, including the piranha, and its floodplains support some of the richest biodiversity on Earth.

docs.annotation_note: Supporta indirettamente la sub-question sull'importanza dell'Amazzonia attraverso il ruolo del fiume nel mantenere l'idrologia della foresta.

docs.summary: The Amazon River maintains the hydrology of the rainforest and is the world's largest river by water discharge.

docs.title: Brazil
docs.text: Brazil is the largest country in South America and the fifth-largest country in the world by both area and population. With a total area of 8.5 million km², it is the largest country in Latin America. Brazil contains the majority of the Amazon rainforest, accounting for approximately 60% of the total forest area. The country has the world's ninth-largest economy. Brazil's official language is Portuguese and its capital is Brasília, while its most populous city is São Paulo.

question: What was the Cold War and when did it end?
answer: The Cold War was a period of geopolitical tension between the United States and the Soviet Union and their respective allies, lasting from approximately 1947 to 1991. It was characterized by ideological rivalry between capitalism and communism, an arms race including nuclear weapons, proxy wars, and a space race. Key events include the Berlin Blockade (1948), the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962), the Vietnam War, and the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan (1979). The Cold War ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union on December 26, 1991, following the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989.

docs.title: Cold War
docs.text: The Cold War was a period of geopolitical tension between the United States and the Soviet Union and their respective allies, the Western Bloc and the Eastern Bloc. It began following the end of World War II in 1947 and lasted until the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991. The conflict was characterized by political and military rivalry, ideological opposition between capitalism and communism, proxy wars in Korea, Vietnam, and elsewhere, and a nuclear arms race. While the two superpowers never engaged in direct warfare, the Cold War shaped world politics for nearly five decades.
docs.evidence_sentence: The Cold War was a period of geopolitical tension between the United States and the Soviet Union. It began following the end of World War II in 1947 and lasted until the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
docs.annotation_note: Documento principale: definisce la Guerra Fredda, la sua durata e le sue caratteristiche principali.
docs.extraction: The Cold War was a US-USSR rivalry from 1947 to 1991, involving ideological conflict, proxy wars, and a nuclear arms race.
docs.summary: The Cold War was a US-USSR geopolitical rivalry (1947–1991) involving ideological conflict, proxy wars, and nuclear arms buildup.

docs.title: Cuban Missile Crisis
docs.text: The Cuban Missile Crisis, also known as the October Crisis, was a confrontation between the United States and the Soviet Union that occurred in October 1962. The crisis began when U.S. reconnaissance aircraft discovered Soviet ballistic missile sites being constructed in Cuba. After a tense 13-day standoff, the Soviet Union agreed to dismantle and remove the missiles in exchange for a U.S. pledge not to invade Cuba. It is widely considered the closest the Cold War came to escalating into full-scale nuclear war.
docs.evidence_sentence: It is widely considered the closest the Cold War came to escalating into full-scale nuclear war.
docs.annotation_note: Risponde alla sub-question sul momento più pericoloso della Guerra Fredda.
docs.extraction: The Cuban Missile Crisis in October 1962 was the closest the Cold War came to nuclear war, ended by Soviet agreement to remove missiles from Cuba.
docs.summary: The Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962) was the most dangerous moment of the Cold War, nearly leading to nuclear conflict.

docs.title: Dissolution of the Soviet Union
docs.text: The dissolution of the Soviet Union was the process of disintegration within the Soviet Union (USSR) which resulted in the end of the country's and the federal government's existence on December 26, 1991. It was a result of the declaration of independence by several Soviet republics and the resignation of Soviet leader Mikhail Gorbachev. The dissolution effectively ended the Cold War and led to the emergence of 15 independent post-Soviet states, the largest of which was the Russian Federation.
docs.evidence_sentence: The dissolution of the Soviet Union resulted in the end of the country's existence on December 26, 1991, effectively ending the Cold War.
docs.annotation_note: Conferma la fine della Guerra Fredda con la dissoluzione dell'URSS il 26 dicembre 1991.
docs.extraction: The Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991, following independence declarations and Gorbachev's resignation, ending the Cold War.
docs.summary: The Soviet Union dissolved on December 26, 1991, ending the Cold War and creating 15 independent states.

docs.title: Berlin Wall
docs.text: The Berlin Wall was a guarded concrete barrier that divided Berlin from 1961 to 1989. It was constructed by the German Democratic Republic (East Germany) to prevent mass emigration to West Germany. The wall became a symbol of the Cold War and the Iron Curtain. On November 9, 1989, following a series of civil unrest events and a miscommunication by an East German official, crowds began to demolish the wall. The fall of the Berlin Wall marked the beginning of the end of the Cold War and the reunification of Germany.
docs.evidence_sentence: The fall of the Berlin Wall on November 9, 1989, marked the beginning of the end of the Cold War.
docs.annotation_note: Supporta la sub-question sulla fine della Guerra Fredda, identificando la caduta del Muro come momento simbolico.
docs.extraction: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, marking the beginning of the end of the Cold War.
docs.summary: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, symbolizing the collapse of communist Eastern Europe and the coming end of the Cold War.

docs.title: Nuclear arms race
docs.text: The nuclear arms race was a competition for supremacy in nuclear warfare between the United States, the Soviet Union, and their respective allies during the Cold War. During this period, in addition to the American and Soviet nuclear stockpiles, other countries developed nuclear weapons, including the United Kingdom, France, and China. The arms race led to the development of the intercontinental ballistic missile (ICBM), nuclear submarines, and the concept of mutually assured destruction (MAD). At its peak in 1986, global nuclear warhead stockpiles numbered approximately 70,000.
